nodes.title: Elem Physic Problem Solving II
nodes.description: Accompanying lecture PHYS 1450. Co-requisite: Concurrent enrollment in PHYS 1450.
nodes.other_requirements: Accompanying lecture PHYS 1450. Co-requisite: Concurrent enrollment in PHYS 1450. S/U grading. PHYS 1450 is a combined lecture/lab. Students should only register for PHYS 1460 if they wish to add an optional problem-solving session to their schedule. Open to Degree and PACE students. Students should have attempted the problems in the current Physics 1450 homework set before attending each class.

nodes.title: Intro to Relativistic Quantum
nodes.description: See Schedule of Courses for specific titles. Prerequisites: Department permission, Graduate student.
nodes.other_requirements: Department permission, Graduate student. Graduate student or undergraduate student with instructor permission. Quantum mechanics I is absolutely necessary as a prerequisite.

nodes.title: Conceptual Physics w/lab
nodes.description: Conceptual survey. Topics selected from mechanics, electricity, magnetism and modern physics. Laboratory content is integrated with the lecture.

nodes.title: Microstructure & Surface Analy
nodes.description: Explores the theory and practical operation of several advanced techniques to analyze the structure, composition, and surfaces of micro and nano-scale materials. Students will be fully trained as users of a Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) including x-ray elemental analysis. Credit will not be given for both PHYS 3175 and PHYS 5185. Prerequisite: PHYS 2500.
nodes.other_requirements: All students must have reliable access to the University of Vermont Brightspace course website. (bb.uvm.edu)

nodes.title: Applictns of Quantum Mechanics
nodes.description: Applications of Quantum Mechanics including Quantum Statistical Mechanics, Time-Independent and Time- Dependent Perturbation Theory, WKB Approximation, Variational Principle and Scattering. Prerequisite: PHYS 3500.
nodes.other_requirements: equivalent

nodes.title: Capstone Seminar
nodes.description: Capstone experience emphasizing the application of physics concepts, development of professional skills, and integration of knowledge acquired during undergraduate Physics studies. Culminating project to demonstrate personal academic and professional growth. Through customized activities, students will explore professional pathways and prepare for their post-college goals. Course complements an approved independent or group study option taken in the same semester. Prerequisites: PHYS 2100, PHYS 2500, PHYS 4100. Co-requisites: PHYS 4991, PHYS 4993, PHYS 4995, or PHYS 4996.
nodes.other_requirements: Co-requisites: PHYS 4991, PHYS 4993, PHYS 4995, or PHYS 4996.

nodes.title: Gr Semiconductor Materials/Dev
nodes.description: Covers Energy band theory, effective mass, band structure and electronic properties of semiconductors. Transport of electrons and holes in bulk materials and across interfaces. MOSFETs, BJTs, pn junctions, and Schottky barriers. Experimental portion of course will have a laboratory component for electronic measurements of semiconductor devices. Credit not awarded for both PHYS 5675 and PHYS 3675. Prerequisite: Graduate student or Instructor permission. Cross-listed with: EE 5440.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate student or Instructor permission

nodes.title: Quantum Mechanics II
nodes.description: Mathematical and physical foundations of nonrelativistic quantum mechanics from the unifying point of view of Dirac. Symmetry operations and the algebraic structure of quantum mechanics are emphasized. Prerequisite: Graduate student or undergraduate student with Instructor permission; knowledge of PHYS 3400 topics strongly recommended.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate student or undergraduate student with Instructor permission; knowledge of PHYS 3400 topics strongly recommended.

nodes.title: Conceptual Physics
nodes.description: One-semester conceptual survey. Topics selected from mechanics, electricity, magnetism and modern physics.

nodes.title: Undergraduate Research
nodes.description: Undergraduate student work on individual or small team research projects under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion. Prerequisites: PHYS 2500; Department permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Department permission

nodes.title: Intro to Cosmology
nodes.description: Topics related to the expanding Universe, including: Kinematics and Dynamics of expansion (space-time curvature, Friedmann equation, etc.), Black-body radiation and the early history of the Universe, the Cosmic Microwave Background, Dark Matter, Structure formation, the Cosmological constant problem, Cosmic Inflation and the early Universe, and basic elements of General Relativity. Prerequisites: PHYS 2500, MATH 2248.

nodes.title: Electrical Engr Concepts
nodes.description: Fundamentals of electrical engineering; DC and AC linear circuit analysis; laboratory component. No credit for more than one of EE 003, EE 2125, EE 2145 and EE 2175. Prerequisites: MATH 1248 or MATH 1242.
nodes.other_requirements: No credit for more than one of EE 003, EE 2125, EE 2145 and EE 2175. Not for BIOM majors; Must register for lab section A01 - A04; No credit for more than one ofEE 2125, EE 2145, orEE 2175; Open to degree and PACE students Please see URL for syllabus from FA 2025. Consider this to be a draft for this coming Fall, as there will be some updates. See above.

nodes.title: Elementary Physics II
nodes.description: Algebra-based survey of electricity, magnetism, optics and modern physics. Appropriate for students in health and life sciences. Counts as a lecture and lab. Optional accompanying problem solving recitation: PHYS 1460. Prerequisites: PHYS 1400, PHYS 1500, or PHYS 1600.
nodes.other_requirements: Must register for labs L01 - L03; Open to Degree and PACE Students

nodes.title: Experimental Physics II
nodes.description: Experiments in classical and modern physics. Prerequisites: PHYS 2500; MATH 2248; Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Junior standing.

nodes.title: Calculus I
nodes.description: Introduction to calculus of functions of one variable including: limits, continuity, techniques and applications of differentiation and integration. Credit not given for more than one course in the pair MATH 1212, MATH 1234 unless followed by MATH 1248 or MATH 1242. No credit following receipt of credit for MATH 1248 or above. Prerequisite: C- or better in MATH 1034 or a score of 76 or higher on the placement assessment.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in MATH 1034 or a score of 76 or higher on the placement assessment. Prereqs enforced by the system.

nodes.title: Nuclear & Elem Particle Physic
nodes.description: Introduction to theoretical and experimental aspects of nuclear and elementary particle physics. Prerequisite: PHYS 2500; Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Junior standing. Basic undergraduate-level knowledge of modern physics, i.e. Quantum Mechanics and atomic physics, as well as basic special relativity (we can review relativity when needed).

nodes.title: Transitional Calculus
nodes.description: Intended to make the transition from MATH 1212 to MATH 2248. Topics are similar to MATH 1248 but recognizing different backgrounds of students in MATH 1212 versus MATH 1234. Credit will not be given for both MATH 1248 and MATH 1242. No credit following receipt of credit for MATH 2248 or above. Prerequisite: B or better in MATH 1212, or B or better in MATH 1224.
nodes.other_requirements: Open to Degree and PACE students

nodes.title: Physics for Engineers I
nodes.description: Mechanics including oscillations and waves. With lab. Accompanying optional problem-solving session: PHYS 1510. Prerequisite: MATH 1234 or MATH 1242.
nodes.other_requirements: School of Engineering students only: CEMS-undeclared, BIOM, CE, EE, EENV, EMGT, ME, CS, DS, MATH; Open to Degree and PACE students;

nodes.title: Elem Physics Problem Solving I
nodes.description: Accompanying lecture PHYS 1400. Co-requisite: Concurrent enrollment in PHYS 1400.
nodes.other_requirements: Co-requisite: Concurrent enrollment in PHYS 1400.

nodes.title: Electronics II
nodes.description: Physical principles of operation of common semiconductor devices. Analog and digital circuits using MOS and bipolar junction transistors. Operational amplifier design. Electronic circuit analysis and simulation. Project-based final. Prerequisite: C- or better in EE 3110.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in EE 3110. Minimum Junior standing. Students are expected to attend and participate in class discussions which are key to meeting the objectives of the course. Students are expected to complete the assigned readings to ensure informed class discussions.

nodes.title: Semiconductor Materials/Devic
nodes.description: Covers Energy band theory, effective mass, band structure and electronic properties of semiconductors. Transport of electrons and holes in bulk materials and across interfaces. MOSFETs, BJTs, pn junctions, and Schottky barriers. Experimental portion of course will cover electronic measurements of semiconductor devices. Credit not awarded for both PHYS 3675 and PHYS 5675. Prerequisites: EE 2145, EE 3410, or PHYS 3300. Cross-listed with: EE 3440.
nodes.other_requirements: Must enroll in PHYS 3675 A01 or A02; In person attendance is required. If you miss more than 3 unexcused classes during the semester you will receive an F in the class.

nodes.title: Nano-analysis of Materials
nodes.description: Explores the theory and practical operation of advanced techniques to analyze the structure, composition, and surfaces of micro and nano-scale materials. Students will be trained as users of a Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) including x-ray elemental analysis. Credit not awarded for both PHYS 3175 and PHYS 5185. Prerequisite: Graduate student in Physics, Materials Science, or related program, or Instructor permission. Cross-listed with: MATS 5185.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate student in Physics, Materials Science, or related program, or Instructor permission.

nodes.title: Master's Thesis Research
nodes.description: Research for the Master's Thesis.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required; Variable credits 1-18

nodes.title: Physics for Engineers II
nodes.description: Electricity, magnetism, electromagnetic waves, optics. Without lab. Accompanying optional problem-solving session: PHYS 1560. Prerequisites: PHYS 1500; MATH 1248 or MATH 1242. Co-requisite: MATH 2248.
nodes.other_requirements: CEMS students only

nodes.title: Electricity & Magnetism
nodes.description: Fundamental principles of electricity and magnetism; electrostatic fields, and magnetic fields of steady currents. Electric and magnetic properties of matter and electromagnetic energy. Prerequisites: PHYS 1650 or PHYS 1550 and MATH 2248. Credit not given for more than one of PHYS 3300 or EE 3100.
nodes.other_requirements: Credit not given for more than one of PHYS 3300 or EE 3100.

nodes.title: Computational Physics I
nodes.description: Introduction to modern computational techniques focusing on the simulation or solution of the behavior of physical systems. Examples will be drawn from classical, statistical, and quantum mechanics, electromagnetism, and chaos. Prerequisites: PHYS 1550 or PHYS 1650; MATH 2248.

nodes.title: Calculus III
nodes.description: Vector-valued functions. Calculus of functions of several variables: partial derivatives, gradient, divergence, curl, multiple integrals, line integrals, Stokes' and Green's theorems. Prerequisite: C- or better in MATH 1248 or MATH 1242.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in MATH 1248 or MATH 1242

nodes.title: Fundamentals of Physics II
nodes.description: Calculus-based introduction to electricity, magnetism and optics. For students in the natural sciences. Laboratory content is integrated with the lecture. Credit not awarded for both PHYS 1550 and PHYS 1650. Prerequisites: PHYS 1500 or PHYS 1600; credit or concurrent enrollment in MATH 1248.
nodes.other_requirements: Credit not awarded for both PHYS 1550 and PHYS 1650. Credit or concurrent enrollment in MATH 1248.

nodes.title: Calculus II
nodes.description: Vectors and vector operations. Techniques and applications of integration. Polar coordinates, Taylor polynomials, sequences and series, power series. Credit will not be given for both MATH 1248 and MATH 1242. No credit following receipt of credit for MATH 2248 or above. Prerequisite: C- or better in MATH 1234.
nodes.other_requirements: C- or better in MATH 1234. C- or better in MATH 021.

nodes.title: Statistical Mechanics
nodes.description: Following a review of thermodynamics, covers the fundamentals of classical and quantum statistical mechanics including ensembles, identical particles, Bose and Fermi statistics, phase-transitions and critical phenomena, renormalization group, irreversible processes and fluctuations. Prerequisite: Graduate student or undergraduate student with Instructor permission; knowledge of PHYS 3400 topics strongly recommended.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate student or undergraduate student with Instructor permission; knowledge of PHYS 3400 topics strongly recommended.

nodes.title: Physics Problem Solving II
nodes.description: Problem-solving techniques for second semester Physics with calculus. Accompanying lecture PHYS 1550.

nodes.title: Undergraduate Research
nodes.description: Undergraduate student work on individual or small team research projects under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.

nodes.title: The Physics of Music
nodes.description: Basic physical principles underlying the production, transmission and perception of musical sound. Vibrations, waves, elementary acoustics with applications to a wide range of musical topics. Prerequisite: High school algebra.
nodes.other_requirements: High school algebra.

nodes.title: Energy and the Environment
nodes.description: Forms of energy as defined in physics; sources, uses, and transformations of energy: introductory seminar will place emphasis on environmental issues. Limited use of algebra and geometry.

nodes.title: Gr Semiconductor Materials/Dev
nodes.description: Covers energy band theory, effective mass, band structure and electronic properties of semiconductors. Transport of electrons and holes in bulk materials and across interfaces. MOSFETs, BJTs, pn junctions, and Schottky barriers. Experimental portion of course will have a laboratory component for electronic measurements of semiconductor devices. Credit not awarded for both EE 5440 and EE 3440. Prerequisite: Graduate student or Instructor permission. Cross-listed with: PHYS 5675.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate student or Instructor permission; Must register for EE 5440 A01 or A02 lab; In person attendance is required. If you miss more than 3 unexcused classes during the semester you will receive an F in the class.

nodes.title: Waves and Quanta
nodes.description: Classical and electromagnetic waves, relativity, wave-particle phenomenology, wave mechanics, and applications of the Schrodinger equation. With laboratory. Prerequisites: PHYS 1650 or PHYS 1550. Co-requisite: MATH 2248.
nodes.other_requirements: Co-requisite: MATH 2248. Students must register for a PHYS 2500 Lab.

nodes.title: Undergraduate Research
nodes.description: Undergraduate student work on individual or small team research projects under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required

nodes.title: Integrated Circuit Fabrication
nodes.description: Science and technology of integrated circuit fabrication. Interaction of processing with material properties, electrical performance, economy, and manufacturability. Study of unit processes used to make semiconductor chips. Prerequisite: PHYS 1550 or PHYS 1650. Cross-listed with: EE 3420.
nodes.other_requirements: Must register for a lab section A01-A02

nodes.title: Classical Mechanics
nodes.description: Newtonian dynamics of particles and systems of particles, with applications to problems of special importance, such as driven and coupled harmonic oscillators and central field trajectories. Prerequisites: PHYS 1650 or PHYS 1550; MATH 2248.
nodes.other_requirements: Students should have access to the textbook, John R. Taylor, Classical Mechanics Second Edition, University Science Books, ISBN 978-1-891389-22-1 (2005). Students will be expected to read the required chapters of the text, turn in Homework Sets approximately weekly, and take a midterm and final examination. Attendance in class is highly recommended.

nodes.title: Fundamentals of Physics I
nodes.description: Calculus-based introduction to kinematics, dynamics, oscillations, thermal physics. For students in the natural sciences. With lab. Credit not awarded for both PHYS 1600 and PHYS 1500. Pre/co-requisite: Credit or concurrent enrollment in MATH 1234.
nodes.other_requirements: Credit or concurrent enrollment in MATH 1234. Open to Degree and PACE students. Credit not awarded for both PHYS 1600 and PHYS 1500.

nodes.title: Intro to Solid State Physics
nodes.description: Introduction to crystal structures, reciprocal lattices, lattice vibrations. Thermal properties of solids and free electron theory of metals and semiconductors. Elementary band theory and introduction to electronic transport theory. Prerequisite: PHYS 2500.
nodes.other_requirements: Students are expected to attend all lectures in person, actively participate in discussions and interactive quizzes, solve at-home problem sets.

nodes.title: Thermal & Statistical Physics
nodes.description: Thermodynamics, kinetic theory, statistical mechanics. Prerequisites: PHYS 1650 or PHYS 1550; MATH 2248.
nodes.other_requirements: A solid foundation in calculus and introductory physics is assumed.

nodes.title: Advanced Dynamics
nodes.description: Classical mechanics presented as the basis of the concepts and methods of modern physics. Variational, Lagrangian, and Hamiltonian formulations, canonical transformations, continuous systems. Prerequisite: Graduate student or undergraduate student with Instructor permission; knowledge PHYS 2200 topics strongly recommended.
nodes.other_requirements: Graduate student or undergraduate student with Instructor permission; knowledge of PHYS 2200 topics strongly recommended.

nodes.title: Doctoral Dissertation Research
nodes.description: Research for the Doctoral Dissertation.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required; Variable credits 1-18

nodes.title: Physics Problem Solving I
nodes.description: Problem-solving techniques for first semester Physics with calculus. Accompanying lecture PHYS 1500.

nodes.title: Experimental Physics I
nodes.description: Classic physics experiments with a strong emphasis on experimental setup, data collection and analysis, error estimation, and writing/presentation of results. The laboratory work is centered around three experiments: Poisson statistics, Cavendish balance, and Kater pendulum. Prerequisite: PHYS 1650 or PHYS 1550.
nodes.other_requirements: This course requires laboratory work of at least 6 hours per week outside of regular class hours. You will be assigned a lab partner or partners in groups of 2 or 3. There will be pre-lab assignments, data collection, lab report writing, homework and presentations. In-class presentations will be individual presentations assigned choosing from a list of topics or based on a mini experiment related to the main experiments. Those will be assigned at least one week in advance of the date of the presentation and will require approximately 4 to 6 hours to prepare. I expect that you are maintaining a good class attendance, prior reading of the assigned material, involving in class discussions and carrying out experiments outside of the class time. I also expect the on-time submission of pre-lab and homework assignments, final lab reports and presentations.

nodes.title: Quantum Mechanics I
nodes.description: Introduction to nonrelativistic quantum mechanics. Schrodinger equation and applications to simple systems. Prerequisite: PHYS 2500, PHYS 2200.

nodes.title: Teaching of College Physics
nodes.description: Instructional strategies and techniques with application to the teaching of laboratories and recitations. Prerequisites: Undergraduate degree in Physics; Instructor permission.
nodes.other_requirements: Undergraduate degree in Physics; Instructor permission.

nodes.title: Intro to Biological Physics
nodes.description: See Schedule of Courses for specific titles.

nodes.title: Elementary Physics I
nodes.description: Algebra-based survey of mechanics, oscillations, waves and thermal physics. Appropriate for students in health and life sciences. Counts as lecture and lab. Optional accompanying problem solving recitation section: PHYS 1410. Prerequisites: High school algebra and trigonometry.
nodes.other_requirements: Must register for PHYS 1400 L01-L04; Open to degree and PACE students
